title: Casting Processes and Discontinuities
narrationScript: Castings are where radiography earns its keep. A casting starts as liquid metal, and everything that goes wrong while that liquid freezes gets locked inside the part, in the volume, exactly where radiography looks. That is why castings are the most radiographed product form in industry, and why a Level Two radiographer needs to understand the foundry, not just the film. If you know how a part was made, you know what can be wrong with it, and you know where to look before you ever hang the first cassette. Start at the beginning of the steel itself. Most metal begins as an ingot, or in a modern mill as a continuously cast strand. When molten steel is poured into an ingot mold it freezes from the walls inward and from the bottom up. Liquid steel shrinks as it solidifies, and the last region to freeze, up at the top center, runs out of liquid to feed it. The result is a funnel shaped cavity called pipe. Dissolved gases come out of solution as the metal freezes and are trapped as blowholes. Oxides, sulfides, and ladle slag are trapped as nonmetallic inclusions. And the elements that stay liquid longest, carbon, sulfur, and phosphorus, concentrate in the last metal to freeze. That uneven chemistry is called segregation. The top of the ingot, with its pipe and its worst segregation, is cropped off and discarded before the rest is rolled into blooms, billets, or slabs. Blooms are the large square sections, billets are the smaller ones, and slabs are wide and flat. Here is the part that matters to us. If the crop is short, the pipe is not removed, and rolling does not heal it. It flattens and stretches into a centerline lamination that can run the length of a plate. Primary discontinuities never die. They only change shape. Now sand casting, the workhorse of the foundry. A pattern shapes a cavity in packed sand. The top half of the mold is the cope, the bottom half is the drag, and they meet at the parting line. Metal pours down the sprue, travels along runners, and enters the cavity through gates. Sand cores form the internal passages, and risers, which are reservoirs of liquid metal sitting over the heavy sections, feed those sections as they shrink. Every one of those features has a failure mode. If a riser is too small or freezes too early, the heavy section it feeds starves, and you get shrinkage. On film, shrinkage looks feathery, branchy, dendritic, or spongy, always dark. Moisture in the sand or a blocked vent makes gas, and gas porosity images as smooth round dark spots. The metal stream can wash sand off a gate and sweep it into the casting as a sand inclusion, an irregular dark patch. If two streams of metal meet after they have cooled too far, they refuse to fuse and leave a cold shut, a smooth dark line with rounded ends. If the metal freezes before the cavity fills at all, that is a misrun. If the mold or a core is too rigid while the casting contracts around it, the metal tears while it is still partly liquid. That is a hot tear, ragged and branched, usually at a fillet or a change of section. And if a core floats or shifts, one wall gets thin and the opposite wall gets thick. On film that is a density difference, darker where the wall is thinner. Centrifugal casting spins a permanent mold while the metal is poured, and the spinning does two useful things. It forces the metal against the mold wall at many times gravity, which feeds shrinkage and builds a dense, sound outer wall. And it drives the light material, slag, dross, and gas, inward to the bore, where it concentrates in the last metal to freeze and is machined away. So a centrifugally cast pipe is usually clean, and the indications that remain crowd near the inside surface. Investment casting, the lost wax process, builds a ceramic shell around a wax pattern, melts the wax out, fires the shell, and pours into it while it is still hot. It produces thin walls, fine detail, and complex shapes, think turbine blades and aerospace hardware, and those same thin sections are its weakness. Metal can freeze before the cavity fills, leaving misruns and cold shuts. Junctions can microshrink. Broken bits of shell or ceramic core become inclusions, and a shifted core thins a wall that radiography is often the only practical way to measure. Learn the process and the film stops being a puzzle. Heavy sections and riser contacts, look for shrink. Cores, look for gas and shift. Gates, look for washed sand. Fillets, look for tears. Thin far ends, look for cold shuts. The casting tells you where to look. Your job is to listen.
imagePrompt: A foundry worker in a silver aluminized heat suit pours glowing molten steel from a ladle into a sand mold flask on a dark casting-house floor, sparks scattering through the smoky air.

sections.heading: Castings: Radiography's Home Turf
sections.body:
A casting is metal that froze into its final shape, and every event of that freezing - the flow of the liquid, the escape of gas, the shrinkage of the metal as it solidified - is recorded inside the part. Discontinuities created at this stage are **inherent discontinuities**: they were born with the material. Most are volumetric - cavities, pores, trapped foreign material - and volumetric flaws are exactly what radiography detects best. That is why castings are the most heavily radiographed product form in industry.

Casting radiography is also usually **graded**, not just accepted or rejected. Code work compares the production film against published **reference radiograph** collections (ASTM sets such as E446 for steel up to about two inches, E186 and E280 for heavier sections, E155 for aluminum, E192 for investment castings) and assigns a severity level to each discontinuity category; the purchase specification then states which levels are acceptable.

For the radiographer the payoff of this lesson is prediction. Casting discontinuities do not scatter at random: shrinkage forms where the last liquid froze, gas collects where moisture or poor venting put it, tears open where the mold restrained contraction. Learn the process and you know where to aim the beam, what image to expect, and how to grade what you find.

sections.heading: Ingots, Blooms, and Billets
sections.body:
Most steel begins as an **ingot** - liquid steel teemed into a cast-iron mold - or, in modern practice, as a continuously cast strand cut into semi-finished shapes. An ingot freezes from the mold walls inward and the bottom upward. Liquid steel shrinks several percent as it solidifies, so the last region to freeze, at the top center, runs out of feed liquid and pulls open a funnel-shaped cavity called **pipe**; a closed cavity lower down is secondary pipe. Gases dissolved in the melt come out of solution and are trapped as **blowholes**. Deoxidation products and ladle slag remain as **nonmetallic inclusions**. Slow-freezing elements - carbon, sulfur, phosphorus - concentrate in the last liquid, a chemical unevenness called **segregation**, worst in the upper center.

The pipe and the heaviest segregation are removed by **cropping** the ingot top before primary rolling. The sound remainder is worked into **blooms** (large, roughly square sections), **billets** (smaller squares rolled down from blooms), and **slabs** (wide, flat sections) - the feedstock for every wrought product. Continuous casting eliminates classical top pipe but can leave centerline segregation and porosity in the strand.

Why it matters downstream: a short crop leaves pipe in the bloom, and rolling does not weld it shut - it flattens and elongates it into a **centerline lamination**. Primary discontinuities are never destroyed by working; they only change shape and orientation.

sections.caption: Primary ingot discontinuities - pipe, blowholes, inclusions, and segregation - and why a short crop sends pipe downstream as centerline lamination.

sections.heading: Sand Casting and Its Discontinuities
sections.body:
Sand casting packs bonded sand around a **pattern**, splits the mold at the **parting line** into the **cope** (top) and **drag** (bottom), and reassembles it with sand **cores** forming the internal passages. Metal enters through the **sprue**, travels along **runners**, and passes through **gates** into the cavity. **Risers** - reservoirs of liquid metal placed over heavy sections - feed the casting as it shrinks, and small metal **chaplets** support cores against flotation. Each feature is also a failure mode:

- **Shrinkage** forms wherever a region freezes after its feed path has closed - heavy sections, junctions, the zone under an undersized riser. It images dark and irregular: cavity, dendritic (branching), filamentary, or sponge-like.
- **Gas porosity** - smooth, round dark spots from mold or core moisture, poor venting, or gassy metal; pinholes cluster near surfaces.
- **Sand and slag inclusions** - irregular dark patches where the stream eroded the mold (a wash) or carried in ladle slag and dross.
- **Cold shut** - two streams met too cold to fuse: a smooth dark line with rounded ends. A **misrun** is the extreme case: the cavity never filled.
- **Hot tears** - ragged, branched dark indications at fillets and section changes, torn open during solidification because a rigid mold or core would not give.
- **Core shift** - a floated or displaced core thins one wall and thickens the other: a wall-to-wall density difference on film. An unfused **chaplet** shows its outline, often a neat circle.

sections.caption: Cross-section of a sand mold showing the cope, drag, gating system, riser, and core, with the origin points of six common casting discontinuities.
Casting discontinuities: cause and radiographic appearance.
rows: Gas porosity / blowholes
Mold or core moisture, poor venting, gassy melt
Smooth round or elliptical dark spots; pinholes cluster near surfaces
Shrinkage (cavity, dendritic, filamentary, sponge)
Section froze after its feed path closed; undersized or misplaced riser
Dark feathery, branching, or mottled sponge-like areas at heavy sections and junctions
Sand / slag inclusion
Mold erosion (wash), loose sand, ladle slag or dross
Irregular dark patches, often near gates
Cold shut
Converging streams too cold to fuse; slow or interrupted pour
Smooth continuous dark line with rounded ends
Misrun
Metal froze before the cavity filled
Missing edge or detail; incomplete shape
Hot tear
Restrained contraction during solidification (rigid mold or core)
Ragged, branched dark line at fillets and section changes
Core shift
Core floated or moved during the pour
One wall darker (thin), opposite wall lighter (thick)
Unfused chaplet
Core support failed to melt into the casting
Faint outline, often circular, at the chaplet location

sections.heading: Centrifugal and Investment Casting
sections.body:
**Centrifugal casting** pours metal into a spinning permanent mold. In true centrifugal casting of pipe and cylinder liners no core is needed: centrifugal force - many times gravity - holds the metal against the mold wall while it solidifies from the outside diameter inward. That force feeds shrinkage continuously, so the outer wall is dense and sound, and it drives the light constituents - slag, dross, oxides, gas - inward to the bore, where they concentrate in the last metal to freeze and are removed by machining the inside diameter. Radiographically, centrifugal castings run clean compared with static castings; the indications that do appear crowd toward the bore, along with banding from speed or vibration variations and occasional hot tears from mold restraint. Semi-centrifugal and centrifuge variants cast wheel-like shapes and clusters of small parts; their centers are the least sound region and are often machined out.

**Investment casting** (the lost-wax process) dips a wax pattern assembly repeatedly in ceramic slurry and stucco, melts out the wax, fires the shell, and pours into the preheated shell. It delivers thin walls, fine detail, and complex near-net shapes - turbine airfoils, aerospace and medical hardware - and such parts are often radiographed one hundred percent. Its weaknesses follow from those thin sections: **misruns and cold shuts** where metal froze before filling, **microshrinkage** at junctions and hot spots, **ceramic shell or core inclusions**, hot tears at restrained junctions, and **core shift**, which thins internal-passage walls that radiography is often the only practical way to verify.

sections.title: Let the process aim your eye
sections.body: Before reading any casting film, put the part mentally back in its mold: **risers and heavy junctions** - expect shrinkage; **cores** - expect gas, shift, and chaplet images; **gates** - expect washed sand; **fillets and section changes** - expect hot tears; **thin sections far from the gate** - expect cold shuts and misruns. Location plus image character identifies the discontinuity.

sections.caption: Simulated radiographs of four casting discontinuity images every Level II must recognize on sight.

figures.title: Inside the ingot: pipe, gas, inclusions, segregation
Sand mold cross-section and discontinuity origins

checkQuestions.question: Why does pipe form at the top center of a conventionally cast ingot?
options: Gas bubbles rise and collect under the mold cap
The last metal to freeze there has no remaining liquid to feed solidification shrinkage
The mold expands at the top during teeming
Slag floats upward and dissolves the metal
checkQuestions.explanation: Steel shrinks several percent as it solidifies; the top center freezes last, runs out of feed liquid, and pulls open a funnel-shaped shrinkage cavity - pipe.

checkQuestions.question: A casting radiograph shows a feathery, branching dark network in a heavy section near a riser contact. The most likely discontinuity is:
options: Gas porosity
A sand inclusion
Dendritic or filamentary shrinkage from a starved feed path
A cold shut
checkQuestions.explanation: Shrinkage forms where a region froze after its feed path closed - heavy sections and riser contacts - and images as feathery, branching, or spongy dark areas, unlike the smooth round spots of gas.

checkQuestions.question: A ragged, branched dark indication runs along the fillet where a thick boss meets a thin web. What is it, and why did it form there?
options: A hot tear - the rigid mold or core restrained contraction at the section change while the metal was still partly liquid
A cold shut - two streams met at the fillet
A crack from rough handling after shakeout
Filamentary shrinkage from a blocked vent
checkQuestions.explanation: Hot tears open during solidification where restrained contraction concentrates strain - typically at fillets and abrupt section changes - and their ragged, branched look distinguishes them from smooth cold shuts.

checkQuestions.question: On a radiograph of a cored casting, one wall images noticeably darker and the opposite wall lighter, with both walls otherwise sound. The cause is most likely:
options: Segregation of heavy elements toward one wall
Core shift - the darker image is the thinner wall
A film processing streak
Backscatter from the floor
checkQuestions.explanation: A floated or shifted core thins one wall and thickens the other; the thin wall absorbs less radiation and images darker, so a wall-to-wall density difference is the classic core-shift signature.

checkQuestions.question: Which statement about investment casting is correct?
options: It packs bonded sand around a reusable wooden pattern
It is limited to heavy sections over two inches thick
Its spinning mold drives slag to the bore for machining
A ceramic shell is built on an expendable wax pattern, and its thin sections are prone to misruns, cold shuts, and microshrinkage
checkQuestions.explanation: Investment (lost-wax) casting builds a fired ceramic shell around a wax pattern and excels at thin, complex parts - which is exactly why freeze-out flaws like misruns, cold shuts, and microshrink at junctions are its typical discontinuities.

references: CP-105-2024 RT Topical Outline, Film Quality and Manufacturing Processes Course §4.1-4.1.4
ASTM reference radiograph collections for castings (e.g., E446, E186, E280, E155, E192) - general reference
General foundry practice references on solidification and casting discontinuities (general reference)
